serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of clay pot rice with braised goby fish and pork fat cracklings is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with braised goby fish pieces and rendered pork fat cracklings.

nutrient_source.title: Clay Pot Rice With Braised Goby Fish And Pork Fat (Cơm Cá Kèo Kho Tóp Mỡ) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010567). Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for white rice, goby fish, and pork fat cracklings in proportions consistent with the dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, cooked (analogous estimate for rice-dominant dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Vietnamese clay pot rice dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked white rice (GI ~72, high category), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100g) / 100 = (72 × 25.19) / 100 = 18.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g from fish sauce and braising liquid, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce in the braising preparation and limit portion size to control sodium intake, while the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (1.4:1) provides partial cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) is the dominant carbohydrate source; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g and protein at 6.5 g/100 g partially moderate postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume this dish in controlled portions (no more than 180–200 g per meal), consider requesting brown rice substitution to lower the glycaemic index, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork fat cracklings contribute saturated fat (~1.4 g/100 g estimated); sodium at 231 mg/100 g is a secondary concern; goby fish provides lean protein and some omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or omit pork fat cracklings to reduce saturated fat intake, request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium, and note that the goby fish component provides beneficial lean protein; total fat at 4.21 g/100 g is moderate and acceptable within a heart-healthy diet when portion-controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork fat cracklings are high in saturated fat which is pro-inflammatory; goby fish provides some anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; fish sauce sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request minimal or no pork fat cracklings, reduce fish sauce seasoning to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and note that the goby fish component and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g support anti-inflammatory gut immune modulation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clay Pot Rice with Braised Goby Fish and Pork Fat Cracklings (Vietnamese: Cơm Cá Kèo Kho Tóp Mỡ) is a traditional Vietnamese comfort dish originating from the Mekong Delta region of southern Vietnam, widely available in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. The dish consists of steamed white rice served in or alongside a clay pot containing goby fish (cá kèo, a small freshwater fish prized for its tender, mildly sweet flesh) that has been slow-braised in a caramelised fish sauce reduction with garlic, shallots, black pepper, and sugar. Rendered pork fat cracklings (tóp mỡ) are added to the braising liquid or served on top, contributing a rich, savoury depth and a contrasting crispy texture. The rice absorbs the braising juices, creating a deeply flavoured, umami-rich one-bowl meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrates, with notable dietary fibre at 3.7 g and potassium at 327 mg. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g, primarily from fish sauce. The high-GI white rice base (GI ~72) means postprandial blood glucose rises are significant; individuals with type 2 diabetes should control portions and consider brown rice substitution. The pork fat cracklings add saturated fat and are a concern for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is a culturally significant everyday meal in Vietnamese cuisine, typically consumed at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010567, confidence 0.95), which is a primary approved source for Singapore-served dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice analogue, clearly documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish, and no sufficiently close composite-dish equivalent exists in USDA FoodData Central to provide reliable estimates for this specific Vietnamese preparation. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB (confirmed zero per HPB source); this is nutritionally unexpected given pork fat content and may reflect the analytical method or specific batch tested — actual cholesterol may be non-zero. 3. saturated_fat_g of 1.4 g/100 g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central component proportions for white rice, goby fish, and pork fat cracklings; not directly reported by HPB. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in approved sources. 5. Glycemic load per serving (100g) calculated as: GL = 72 × (25.19) / 100 = 18.1; gi_category is high. 6. The dish contains pork and is not halal. 7. This dish is Vietnamese cuisine served in Singapore; the validator flagged missing Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names. Although the dish is Vietnamese in origin, it is served in Singapore and the record is tagged region_tags: SG in the input; accordingly, Singaporean multilingual naming rules have been applied and Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases have been added to satisfy G6 and Q8. 8. Chinese simplified: 砂锅鱼煮饭配猪油渣; Chinese traditional: 砂鍋魚煮飯配豬油渣; Malay: Nasi Periuk Tanah dengan Ikan Gobi Rebus dan Kerak Lemak Babi; Tamil: களிமண் பாத்திரத்தில் சமைத்த சோறு கோபி மீன் மற்றும் பன்றி கொழுப்பு; Hindi: मिट्टी के बर्तन में पकाया चावल, ब्रेज़्ड गोबी मछली और सूअर की चर्बी के साथ.
